standard_description:
Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to whole numbers less than or equal to 1,000,000.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
How much is a million?
Problem 2
Make a model to represent the size of 1,000,000.
Problem 3
a.   Use a place value chart to model the size of 1,000,000, building it from bundles of 10 starting with the ones place.
b.   Use your work in Part (a) to solve each of the following:
10
$$\times$$
10 = ______________
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10 = ______________
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
10 = ______________
c.   Use your work in Part (a) to write each of the following as a product of tens:
1,000 = ____________________________
100,000 = ____________________________
100,000,000 = ____________________________

Problem 1
A dime is
$${{1\over10}}$$
of a dollar and a penny is
$${{1\over100}}$$
of a dollar.
Would you rather have 4 one-dollar bills and 1 dime, 42 dimes, or 413 pennies? Justify your answer.
Problem 2
a.   Decide whether each of these has the same value as 3.57. Explain your reasoning.
357 tenths
357 hundredths
3 ones 57 tenths
3 ones 57 hundredths
34 tenths 17 hundredths
3 ones 7 hundredths 5 tenths
b.   Find as many ways as you can to make
$$2 \frac{4}{10}$$
using ones, tenths, and hundredths. If you think you have found all the ways, explain how you know your list is complete.
Problem 3
a.   What number represents the same amount as 1 one 70 hundredths?
b.   What number represents the same amount as 40 tenths 9 hundredths?
c.   What number represents the same amount as 268 hundredths?
d.   What number represents the same amount as 2 ones 14 tenths?
e.   What number represents the same amount as 3 ones 16 tenths 25 hundredths?

Complete the following chart:
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
a.   Ms. Britton bought a bottle of water at Stop and Shop. She didn’t have any cash so she wrote a check to pay for it. How should she write the cost in the red box?
###IMAGE0###
b.   Use similar reasoning to write the following values in standard and expanded form.
Four thousand nine and seventy-three thousandths
Thirty-five and six hundred eight thousandths
Problem 2
Write each of the following in expanded and word form.
a.   25.403
b.   1,028.04
c.   9.795
Problem 3
Write each of the following in standard and word form.
a.
$${\left(4 \times 100\right) + \left(5 \times {1\over100}\right) + \left(9 \times {1\over1,000}\right)}$$
b.
$${ 1 \times 10 + 3 \times 1 + 4 \times 0.1 + 7 \times 0.01}$$
c.
$${\left(2 \times {1\over10}\right) + \left(0\times {1\over100}\right) + \left(8 \times {1\over1,000}\right)}$$

Fill out the following chart.
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
a.   Look at the number line below:
###IMAGE0###
Based on where 0 and 100 are, what number do you think the question mark is on? Explain your choice.
b.   Look at the number line below:
###IMAGE1###
Based on where 0 and 1,000 are, what number do you think the question mark is on? Explain your choice.
Problem 2
a.   What is the location of the following point?
###IMAGE2###
b.   What is the location of the following point?
###IMAGE3###
Problem 3
a.   Plot 27,361 on a number line from 27,360 to 27,370.
b.   Plot 27,361 on a number line from 27,300 to 27,400.
c.   Plot 27,361 on a number line from 27,000 to 28,000.
d.   Plot 27,361 on a number line from 20,000 to 30,000.

Problem 1
Write 500,000 + 20,000 + 6,000 + 100 + 4 in standard and word form.
Problem 2
Write three hundred forty thousand, seventy-eight in standard and expanded form.
Problem 3
Write 73,906 in word and expanded form.

Task
Arrange these numbers in increasing order, beginning with the least.
$$2400 \qquad 4002 \qquad 2040 \qquad 420 \qquad 2004$$
Arrange these numbers in decreasing order, beginning with the greatest.
$$1470 \qquad 847 \qquad 710 \qquad 1047 \qquad 147$$

Problem 1
Jeremy says he can write 1,456 as 14 thousands 56 ones. Is he correct? Why or why not?
Problem 2
Fill out the following table.
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
Look at your paper base ten blocks. The ones piece is the smallest square. Then tens piece is a 10 × 1 strip. The hundreds piece is the larger 10 × 10 square.
a.   Use the paper base ten blocks to construct 1,000. Use tape as needed.
b.   Use the paper base ten blocks to construct 10,000. Use tape as needed.
c.   What comes next? How much bigger will it be?
Problem 2
a.   Look at the ones, tens, hundreds, and thousands base ten blocks.
What would you expect a ten thousands base ten block to look like?
What would you expect a hundred thousands base ten block to look like?
What comes next? What would you expect its base ten block to look like?
b.   What pattern do you notice in the shapes of the base ten blocks? What pattern do you notice in the names of the place values? ​​​​​​
Problem 3
When we write really large numbers, writing commas in between groups of them can help make it easier to write them. We place commas every three places from the right, starting with the ones place. For example, we write 5,678 with the comma placed three places from the right.
a.   Write 430325 in standard form with the correct placement of commas. Then read the number name out loud.
b.   Write 3 hundred thousands 2 thousands 4 hundreds 5 tens 7 ones in standard form with the correct placement of commas. Then read the number name out loud.
c.   Read 50,038 out loud. Then write it in unit form.

Problem 1
What number comes after 1,000? What is the next number after 9,999?
Problem 2
a.   1,236 is a number.
Build the number with base ten blocks or draw a picture to represent it.
Write it as a sum of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.
Write its name in unit form.
b.   5,078 is a number.
Build the number with base ten blocks or draw a picture to represent it.
Write it as a sum of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.
Write its name in unit form.

Complete the chart.
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
Complete the following table. Draw models for each row if they will help you.
###TABLE0###
Problem 2
Use the following numbers to answer each of the following questions:
1.57
5.03
98.30
623.46
a.   What is the value of the digit 1 in (i)? The digit 5?
b.   What is the value of the digit 3 in (ii), (iii), and (iv) above? How are those values related to one another?
c.   What is the value of the digit in the hundreds place in (iv)? What is the value of the digit in the hundredths place in (iv)? How are these similar? How are they different?
Explain how knowing that 2 thousands 379 ones is equivalent to 2,379 ones helps you determine the number of grams equivalent to 2 kg 379 g.

Problem 1
Solve.
17 thousands 50 ones = _________ ones
17 kg 50 g = _________ g
Solve.
10 thousands 567 ones = _________ ones
10 L 567 mL = _________mL
Solve.
9 hundreds 2 ones = _________ ones
9 m 2 cm = _________ cm
What do you notice about the relationship between Parts (i) and (ii) in each of the problems above? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
Place the following quantities on the number line below.
###TABLE0###
###IMAGE0###
List the quantities in order from least to greatest.

Problem 1
Use the number below to answer the following questions.
268.39
a.   What digit is in the hundreds place? What is its value?
b.   What digit is in the tens place? What is its value?
c.   What digit is in the ones place? What is its value?
d.   What digit is in the tenths place? What is its value?
e.   What digit is in the hundredths place? What is its value?
Problem 2
Complete the following chart.
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
Using blank hundreds charts, create a book that represents numbers 1 to 1,000.
Problem 2
a.   Find 249 in your 1,000 book. Which hundreds page is it on? Which two benchmarks tens is it in between? Which ten is it closer to?
b.   I'm thinking of a number. It's on the 400 page. It's close to the number 460. It ends in 2. What number am I thinking of?

Problem 1
Fill in the blanks below to make the following statements true.
a.   5 ones 7 tenths = ________ tenths = ________ hundredths
b.   4 ones 3 tenths 8 hundredths = ________ tenths ________ hundredths = ________ hundredths
c.   260 hundredths = ________ tenths = ________ ones ________ tenths
d.   901 hundredths = ________ tenths ________ hundredths = ________ ones ________ tenths ________ hundredths
Problem 2
Write each of the values from #1 in decimal form below.
a.   5 ones 7 tenths
b.   4 ones 3 tenths 8 hundredths
c.   260 hundredths
d.   901 hundredths

Problem 2

Problem 1
Write the following numbers in standard form. Include commas where appropriate.
a.   6 thousands 4 tens 9 ones
b.   7 ten thousands 2 thousands 1 hundred 5 tens 3 ones
c.   8 hundred thousands 4 ten thousands 7 hundreds 9 tens 2 ones
Problem 2
Write the following numbers in unit form.
a.   23,091
b.   8,530
c.   360,467

Problem 1
a.   A man in Massachusetts won the lottery yesterday. Look how much he won!
###IMAGE0###
The lottery made a mistake when they printed his check, though—it’s missing a dollar amount! What number should be written in that box?
b.   Use similar reasoning to write the following values in standard and expanded form.
Two hundred seventy thousand eight hundred fifty
Sixty-four thousand three
Problem 2
Write each of the following in expanded and word form.
a.   23,984
b.   601,408
c.   7,056
Problem 3
Write each of the following in standard and word form.
a.
$$500,000 + 7,000 + 800 + 60 + 3$$
b.
$$(8\times 100,000) + (4 \times 10,000) + (1 \times 10) + (2 \times 1)$$
c.
$$9 \times 1,000 + 7 \times 100 + 6 \times 10 + 0 \times 1$$

Problem 1
Ms. Glynn wants to model the number 2,213 with base ten blocks, but she only has 1 thousand block, as shown below. She has lots of hundreds, tens, and ones blocks.
###IMAGE0###
How can she make this number using the blocks she has?
Problem 2
a.   What number represents the same amount as 14 hundreds 23 tens?
b.   What are two different ways you could represent 5,641?

Problem 1
Mr. Wynn gets some butcher paper from the teacher supply room to put down the length of the hallway. The butcher paper is 3 feet tall and 30 feet long. When he puts it up in the hallway, he realizes he needs a little more to fully cover the wall. The extra piece he needed is 3 feet tall and 2 feet long.
a.   How many square feet of butcher paper did Mr. Wynn put in the hallway?
b.   What was the total length of width of the space Mr. Wynn ended up covering?
Problem 2
a.   Estimate the product 57
$$\times$$
9.
b.   The area model below represents 57
$$\times$$
9.
###IMAGE0###
Label each region of the area model with its respective area.
Find the value of 57
$$\times$$
9. Show your reasoning.
Problem 3
a.   Solve each of the following word problems. Show or explain your work.
In the 2017–2018 school year, Match Community Day had 61 students in each grade level. How many students were at Match Community Day that year if the school had Kindergarten and Grades 1–4?
A club’s first meeting was attended by 28 people. The second meeting was attended by 4 times as many people as the first meeting. How many people attended the second meeting?
b.   Did you use an area model to represent either or both problems in Part (a)? If not, could you have? Why or why not?

c.   Given 2,540 and 23,805, which one is larger? Use <, >, or = to record your comparison.
